us-politics.headline: Trump Cabinet in flux as Bondi, Noem exit raises shake-up fears
us-politics.body:
The departures of Pam Bondi and Kristi Noem have unsettled Washington, with Trump privately weighing further Cabinet changes while telling allies he wants to avoid a wholesale overhaul. Todd Blanche, Trump's former personal attorney, has stepped into the attorney general role, carrying both the president's trust and the weight of his expectations at a politically charged Justice Department.

Supporters argue the moves reflect Trump's preference for loyalty and results. Critics see instability at the top of government. The Hill and Washington Post both report Trump is eyeing additional reshuffles, with Commerce Secretary Lutnick and others potentially in the frame.

sources.title: Bondi, Noem ousters raise questions about future Cabinet shake-ups
Trump weighs more Cabinet changes, but wants to avoid ‘massive shake-up’
Todd Blanche takes on DOJ’s top job and the burden of Trump’s expectations
Week in Politics: Trump's latest speech; SCOTUS on birthright citizenship; Pam Bondi

us-politics.headline: Trump signs college sports order; floats shifting day care to states
us-politics.body:
Trump signed an executive order targeting college sports reform, his second such move, directing changes to athlete compensation and eligibility rules that have divided the NCAA era. Supporters say federal intervention breaks a long-running logjam. Critics question whether presidential orders are the right tool for governing amateur athletics.

Separately, Trump floated shifting federal day care policy to states, framing it as a return of local control. Child care advocates pushed back hard, warning that state-level variation would leave low-income families without reliable support. Both moves fit a broader pattern of using executive action to reshape domestic policy quickly, bypassing Congress.

sources.title: Trump signs order aimed at reforming college sports
Trump again orders changes to ‘save’ college sports
Trump floats letting states handle day care. Advocates call it a lead balloon

us-politics.headline: Voting rights, SCOTUS, and Oregon's mail-ballot lawsuit dominate legal front
us-politics.body:
Oregon's attorney general filed suit against Trump's executive order restricting vote-by-mail, calling it a source of confusion, chaos, and distrust in the electoral system. The state argues the order conflicts with existing federal law and disenfranchises voters who rely on mail ballots. The administration contends the measure protects election integrity.

Meanwhile, the Supreme Court is weighing Trump's push to end birthright citizenship, a case with sweeping constitutional implications. NPR's political roundup flagged both issues as defining legal battles of the current term, with lower courts so far blocking the birthright order. The outcomes will shape voting access and citizenship law for years.

sources.title: ‘Confusion, chaos and distrust’: Oregon challenges Trump’s order restricting vote-by-mail
Week in Politics: Trump's latest speech; SCOTUS on birthright citizenship; Pam Bondi

us-politics.headline: World leaders sideline Trump on Iran; retirement savings data worries economists
us-politics.body:
Major world leaders are coordinating responses to rising tensions in the Strait of Hormuz without directly involving the Trump administration, according to The Hill. The move signals growing unease among allies about Washington's reliability as a diplomatic partner, though the White House has not publicly commented on being excluded from the talks.

On the domestic economic front, a new payroll firm report shows Americans are saving less for retirement, a trend analysts link to inflation, stagnant wages, and rising costs. The data lands as the administration promotes its economic record. Economists across the political spectrum treat the erosion of retirement savings as a structural warning sign, not a partisan one.

sources.title: World leaders bypass Trump to tackle Strait of Hormuz crisis
Warning sign? Retirement saving habits eroding, payroll firm says

au-politics.headline: Pezzullo Report Reveals 'Reckless' Ties to Liberal Powerbroker
au-politics.body:
A confidential report has found former Home Affairs secretary Michael Pezzullo acted recklessly in his dealings with a Liberal party powerbroker, adding fresh detail to the conduct that led to his sacking in 2023. The findings raise ongoing questions about the relationship between senior public servants and political operatives, and how far that line can be pushed before accountability kicks in.

Pezzullo was one of Australia's most powerful bureaucrats. The report's language is pointed - reckless is not a mild finding - and it will likely renew debate about oversight mechanisms for senior officials across the public service.

sources.title: Confidential report found former home affairs boss Michael Pezzullo was ‘reckless’ in engagement with Liberal powerbroker

au-politics.headline: Fuel Supply Fears Squeeze Farmers and Motorists Alike
au-politics.body:
Energy Minister Chris Bowen says fewer service stations are running dry as Australia's fuel supply stabilises, but pressure on the sector is far from over. Farmers are calling on supermarkets to absorb higher transport costs by paying more for produce, warning that fuel price volatility is cutting into already thin margins.

The competing demands put retailers in a difficult position. Supermarkets, already under political scrutiny over pricing practices, now face calls to effectively subsidise agricultural supply chains. Whether they move voluntarily or wait for regulatory pressure remains to be seen.

sources.title: Fewer service stations running out of petrol and diesel as Australia’s fuel supplies remain strong, energy minister says
Supermarkets asked to pay more for produce to absorb fuel costs for farmers

au-politics.headline: Murder Charges, Missing Persons and a Major Drug Bust
au-politics.body:
Victorian police arrested two people in connection with the disappearance of Dezi Freeman, while a separate man was charged with murder after being intercepted attempting to board an international flight. A woman was also charged with murder following the death of a 24-year-old in Laverton. Three unrelated cases, all moving fast.

In a major organised crime operation, nine people face charges over an alleged conspiracy to import tonnes of cocaine and methamphetamine via a so-called mother ship in Australian waters. Federal authorities say the scale of the alleged operation is significant. Police are also conducting a full search for a missing woman near The Gap in Albany, Western Australia.

sources.title: Man trying to board international flight charged with murder
Victoria police arrest two people as part of Dezi Freeman investigation
Two arrested as part of investigation into movements of Dezi Freeman
Woman charged with murder following investigation into death of 24yo
Nine charged over alleged conspiracy to import tonnes of cocaine and meth via ‘mother ship’ in Australian waters
Police launch full-scale search to locate woman missing in popular tourism spot

au-politics.headline: Victorian Election Heats Up as Challenger Targets Premier Allan
au-politics.body:
Andrew Lethlean has confirmed he will contest Bendigo East, taking direct aim at Victorian Premier Jacinta Allan in her own seat at the next state election. The move signals opposition confidence in a seat Allan has held comfortably, and raises the stakes for a premier already navigating a difficult political environment.

Challenging a sitting premier in their home electorate is a high-risk, high-visibility strategy. It rarely succeeds but forces the premier to campaign locally rather than statewide. Whether Lethlean can make the contest genuinely competitive will be watched closely as the election approaches.

sources.title: Lethlean to challenge Victorian premier in Bendigo East

world.headline: US-Israel War on Iran Enters Day 36 With Rising Casualties
world.body:
US and Israeli forces continued strikes on Iran as the conflict entered its 36th day, with Iranian missiles hitting residential areas in central Israel overnight. A US airman is missing after an incident inside Iran, prompting a search operation that rescuers describe as harrowing and dangerous.

Iran executed two members of a banned opposition group amid the fighting, a move critics say is designed to suppress dissent under cover of wartime. The conflict is reshaping regional politics rapidly, with no ceasefire in sight and civilian infrastructure on both sides taking direct hits.

sources.title: What we know so far about the search for missing US airman in Iran
Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member
Iran executes two convicted members of banned opposition group
Iran war: What is happening on day 36 of US-Israeli attacks?
Iranian missile attack hits residential areas in central Israel

world.headline: Russia Strikes Ukraine Over Easter, Rejecting Ceasefire
world.body:
Russia launched fresh attacks on Ukraine over the Easter weekend, killing at least four people. President Zelensky accused Moscow of deliberately choosing escalation over a ceasefire, framing the timing as a deliberate provocation against Ukrainian civilians and the country's Christian majority.

The strikes follow repeated calls from Kyiv and Western allies for a temporary halt to fighting. Russia has not responded to ceasefire proposals.

sources.title: At least four people killed in Russian attacks on Ukraine
Russia chose 'Easter escalation' over ceasefire, says Zelensky

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Reaches Halfway Point to the Moon
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew passed the halfway mark on their lunar flyby mission, releasing striking images of Earth taken from deep space. The photographs, described by the crew as spectacular, mark the farthest humans have traveled from Earth in over five decades.

The mission is a crewed test flight, not a landing, but represents a major step toward returning astronauts to the lunar surface. NASA released the images publicly as the spacecraft continues its outbound trajectory.

sources.title: Artemis II crew now halfway to Moon as they take 'spectacular' image of Earth
NASA unveils first images of Earth from Artemis II

world.headline: Trump Pushes Alcatraz Reopening and Wilderness Drilling
world.body:
President Trump requested $152 million to reopen Alcatraz as a functioning federal prison, reviving the long-shuttered island facility in San Francisco Bay. The proposal signals a broader push toward harsher incarceration policy and follows a series of aggressive domestic moves from the administration.

Separately, Trump moved to open protected wilderness areas to oil and gas extraction, fulfilling a core campaign pledge. The decision drew sharp criticism from environmental groups and puts large tracts of federal land at risk of industrial development.

sources.title: Trump seeks $152m to reopen notorious Alcatraz prison
'Drill baby drill': Trump opens wilderness to big energy

ai.headline: Anthropic's Big Week: Biotech Deal, PAC, and Claude Controversy
ai.body:
Anthropic is moving fast on multiple fronts. The company reportedly acquired biotech startup Coefficient Bio for $400 million, launched a political action committee to expand its Washington influence, and is riding high in private markets - though SpaceX's anticipated IPO may soon compete for the same investor dollars.

Not everything went smoothly. Anthropic effectively cut off third-party Claude client OpenClaw by requiring subscribers to pay extra to use it, drawing sharp criticism from users who saw it as a de facto ban. Meanwhile, hackers exploited a Claude Code leak, bundling the stolen code with malware and distributing it online. On a brighter note, Claude Code independently uncovered a Linux vulnerability that had gone undetected for 23 years.

sources.title: Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware
Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Claude Code Found a Linux Vulnerability Hidden for 23 Years
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw
OpenClaw gives users yet another reason to be freaked out about security
Anthropic buys biotech startup Coefficient Bio in $400M deal: Reports
Anthropic ramps up its political activities with a new PAC

ai.headline: OpenAI Reshuffles Leadership as Key Executives Step Back
ai.body:
OpenAI is reorganizing at the top. COO Brad Lightcap is moving into a new "special projects" role, while Fidji Simo, who joined less than a year ago to run the company's consumer products, is taking medical leave. The head of OpenAI's AGI division is also stepping away temporarily.

The simultaneous departures and role changes raise questions about internal stability at a company already navigating a high-stakes transition to a for-profit structure. OpenAI has not detailed what triggered the shuffle or who will cover the vacated responsibilities in the interim.

sources.title: OpenAI executive shuffle includes new role for COO Brad Lightcap to lead ‘special projects’
OpenAI’s AGI boss is taking a leave of absence
OpenAI’s Fidji Simo Is Taking Medical Leave Amid an Executive Shake-Up

ai.headline: AI's Power Hunger Fuels Gas Plants and Data Center Stumbles
ai.body:
AI companies are building large natural gas plants to feed their data center appetites, bypassing cleaner alternatives in a race for reliable power. Critics warn the buildout locks in fossil fuel infrastructure for decades and exposes companies to regulatory and climate risk.

The Trump administration's parallel push to accelerate AI data center construction is running into a self-inflicted problem: its own tariffs are raising the cost of the hardware and materials needed to build them. The contradiction is slowing projects the White House publicly champions.

sources.title: Trump ignores biggest reasons his AI data center buildout is failing
AI companies are building huge natural gas plants to power data centers. What could go wrong?

ai.headline: Artemis II Heads to the Moon as NASA Budget Faces Cuts
ai.body:
The Artemis II crew is en route to the Moon and, by all accounts, the mission is going well. The astronauts have already captured striking images of Earth from deep space, marking the first crewed lunar flyby in over 50 years.

The timing is awkward for NASA. The Trump administration has proposed steep budget cuts to the agency even as Artemis II is mid-flight. The cuts would hit future missions hardest, putting the long-term Artemis program in an uncertain position despite the current mission's success.

sources.title: Trump proposes steep cut to NASA budget as astronauts head for the Moon
As Artemis II zooms to the Moon, everything seems to be going swimmingly
Artemis II crew take “spectacular” image of Earth

btc.headline: Bitcoin Market Thins as Big Traders Bleed Billions
btc.body:
Five separate data sources point to the same problem: Bitcoin's market liquidity is quietly deteriorating. Order books are getting shallower, and the depth needed to absorb large trades is shrinking - a warning sign that volatility could spike on relatively small moves.

The pain is already showing up in trading results. Wealthy Bitcoin traders lost an average of $337 million per day through Q1 2026, a figure that reflects both poor positioning and a market that punishes size. Analysts watching price action say Bitcoin's prolonged directionless consolidation could resolve into a sharp breakout, but thinning liquidity means that move - up or down - may be more violent than expected.

sources.title: Five data sources say the same thing about bitcoin market. It's thinning from the inside
Rich Bitcoin traders lost $337M daily in first quarter of 2026
Bitcoin’s ‘no direction’ action may lead to heavier breakout: Analyst

btc.headline: Quantum Threat Looms Over Crypto's Fastest Blockchains
btc.body:
Quantum computing's threat to blockchain security is moving from theoretical to urgent. A recent analysis found that "cracking" Bitcoin in nine minutes - a claim circulating from quantum research - does not mean wallets are at immediate risk, but it does expose how little time developers may have to act once quantum hardware matures.

Solana's attempt to address the threat reveals a brutal tradeoff: post-quantum cryptographic algorithms are computationally heavy, and bolting them onto a network built for speed would gut its performance. No clean solution exists yet. The pressure is building on developers across chains to find one before the hardware catches up.

sources.title: Solana's quantum-threat readiness reveals harsh tradeoff: security vs speed
Here's what 'cracking' bitcoin in 9 minutes by quantum computers actually means

btc.headline: Bitcoin ETFs Set to Eclipse Gold, Tether Eyes $500B Raise
btc.body:
Analyst James Seyffart argues Bitcoin ETFs will eventually surpass gold ETFs in size, citing Bitcoin's expanding use cases and accelerating institutional adoption. Gold ETFs took decades to build their base. Bitcoin ETFs are moving faster.

Meanwhile, Tether is weighing a fundraising round at a $500 billion valuation but may delay if investor demand falls short of expectations. The hesitation signals that even the stablecoin giant is reading a cautious market. Together, both stories reflect the same tension: long-term institutional confidence in crypto is growing, but short-term appetite for risk remains fragile.

sources.title: Tether may delay fundraising if demand falls short at $500B valuation: Report
Bitcoin ETFs 'will be larger' than gold ETFs: Analyst

btc.headline: Prediction Markets Face Courts, Controversy, and Backlash
btc.body:
Polymarket pulled a prediction market tied to a missing U.S. pilot after public backlash, highlighting the reputational risk that comes with betting on real human tragedies. The platform offered no detailed explanation. The removal was swift.

Kalshi is fighting a separate battle in Nevada, where a judge extended a ban on its sports prediction markets. The ruling keeps Kalshi out of one of the country's most active gambling states and signals that regulators are not ready to treat prediction markets as a clean alternative to traditional sports betting. Both cases show the sector is still navigating where legal and ethical lines fall.

sources.title: Polymarket takes down market on missing US pilot after backlash
Judge continues Nevada ban on Kalshi sports markets

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Mushroom Galette
food.description: A free-form buckwheat pastry tart filled with caramelised leeks and shiitake mushrooms lacquered in white miso butter
food.tip: Chill the buckwheat dough for at least 30 minutes before rolling — it's more fragile than standard shortcrust and cold fat is what keeps it from crumbling apart

exercise.name: The April Ladder
exercise.equipment: A single kettlebell or dumbbell, timer
exercise.description: A ascending-descending ladder circuit — swings, goblet squats, and push-ups starting at 2 reps each and climbing to 10 then back down to 2 — with no fixed rest, just move at a pace that lets you finish each rung with clean form

learn.topic: Strandbeests — Theo Jansen's kinetic wind-powered creatures
learn.description: Dutch artist-engineer Theo Jansen has spent decades building large skeletal sculptures from PVC tubing that walk autonomously along beaches powered only by wind, using a linkage mechanism he evolved through computer simulation
learn.why: The project sits at a genuinely strange crossroads of art, evolutionary computation, and mechanical engineering — and studying Jansen's 11-bar linkage system will quietly change how you think about the boundary between life and machine
